reasoning: All four reach the same sound verdict (decline as structured, counter-offer to ring-fence the team, secure upfront/milestone cash, demand reusability/IP), so discrimination rests on rigor, calibration, and bias-handling. A wins: it carries the runway math all the way through ($60k burn, ~$100k true net cash, ~$300k deferred onto the customer, 5-6 month extension), explicitly invokes outside-view base rates and the inside-view discount, adds a fragility/barbell check and a premortem, lists concrete falsifiers, and is the only response to attach an explicit, justified confidence (~70%) plus the facts that would move it. C is a close second and arguably the deepest on the opportunity-cost mechanism (30 person-months framing, "compared to what?" test, a correct sunk-cost check) but offers no explicit probability and leaks an internal "Relevant files applied" artifact. D makes the sharpest single bias call (attribute substitution) and uses systems archetypes well, but undercuts its own rigor by leaving the burn figure as an unfilled "$X/month" placeholder. B is solid on second/third-order effects and negotiation terms but is the least quantified (declines to estimate burn), names biases only implicitly, and gives no explicit confidence, landing it last.
